questions.question:
A depth-first search is performed on a directed acyclic graph. Let d[u] denote the time at which vertex u is visited for the first time and f[u] the time at which the DFS call to the vertex u terminates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE for all edges (u, v) in the graph ?

(GATE IT 2007)

questions.question:
Which of the following graphs has an Eulerian circuit?

(GATE CSE 2007)
options:
A.

Any k-regular graph where k is an even number.

C.

The complement of a cycle on 25 vertices.
questions.correct_answer:
C.

The complement of a cycle on 25 vertices.

questions.question:
Consider the depth-first-search of an undirected graph with 3 vertices P, Q, and R. Let discovery time d(u) represent the time instant when the vertex u is first visited, and finish time f(u) represent the time instant when the vertex u is last visited. Given that
\(\begin{array}{l|l}\hline \text{$d(P) = 5$ units } & \text{ $f(P) = 12$ units } \\\hline \text{$d(Q) = 6$ units} & \text{ $f(Q) = 10$ units} \\\hline \text{$d(R) = 14$ unit} & \text{$f(R) = 18$ units} \\\hline \end{array}\)
Which one of the following statements is TRUE about the graph?

(GATE IT 2006)
options:
B.

There are two connected components, and P and R are connected

C.

There are two connected components, and Q and R are connected

D.

There are two connected components, and P and Q are connected
questions.correct_answer:
D.

There are two connected components, and P and Q are connected
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Which of the following is the correct decomposition of the directed graph given below into its strongly connected components?



(GATE IT 2006)
options:
A.

\(\left \{ P, Q, R, S \right \}, \left \{ T \right \},\left \{ U \right \}, \left \{ V \right \}\)

B.

\(\left \{ P,Q, R, S, T, V \right \}, \left \{ U \right \}\)

C.

\(\left \{ P, Q, S, T, V \right \}, \left \{ R \right \},\left \{ U \right \}\)

D.

\(\left \{ P, Q, R, S, T, U, V \right \}\)
questions.correct_answer:
B.

\(\left \{ P,Q, R, S, T, V \right \}, \left \{ U \right \}\)

questions.question:
Let T be a depth first search tree in a undirected graph G Vertices u and v are leaves of this tree T. The degrees of both u and v in G are at least 2. Which one of the following statements is true?

(GATE CSE 2006)
options:
A.

There must exist a vertex w adjacent to both u and v in G

B.

There must exist a vertex w whose removal disconnects u and v in G

C.

There must be exist a cycle in G containing u and v

D.

There must exist a cycle in G containing u and all its neighbours in G
questions.correct_answer:
D.

There must exist a cycle in G containing u and all its neighbours in G
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
Let SHAM3 be the problem of finding a Hamiltonian cycle in a graph G=(V,E) with |V| divisible by 3 and DHAM3 be the problem of determining if a Hamltonian cycle exists in such graphs. Which one of the following is true?

(GATE CSE 2006)
options:
B.

SHAM3 is NP-hard, but DHAM3 is not

C.

DHAM3 is NP-hard, but SHAM3 is not

questions.question:
A sink in a directed graph is a vertex i such that there is an edge from every vertex \(j \neq i\) to i and there is no edge from i to any other vertex. A directed graph G with n vertices is represented by its adjacency matrix A, where A[i] [j] = 1 if there is an edge directed from vertex i to j and 0 otherwise. The following algorithm determines whether there is a sink in the graph G.

 i = 0;
do {
    j = i + 1;
    while ((j < n) && E1) j++;
    if (j < n) E2;
} while (j < n);
flag = 1;
for (j = 0; j < n; j++)
    if ((j! = i) && E3) flag = 0;
if (flag) printf("Sink exists") ;
else printf ("Sink does not exist");

Choose the correct expression for \(E_3\).

(GATE IT 2005)
questions.explanation: Click here for detail solution by gateoverflow

questions.question:
A sink in a directed graph is a vertex i such that there is an edge from every vertex \(j \neq i\) to i and there is no edge from i to any other vertex. A directed graph G with n vertices is represented by its adjacency matrix A, where A[i] [j] = 1 if there is an edge directed from vertex i to j and 0 otherwise. The following algorithm determines whether there is a sink in the graph G.

 i = 0;
do {
    j = i + 1;
    while ((j < n) && E1) j++;
    if (j < n) E2;
} while (j < n);
flag = 1;
for (j = 0; j < n; j++)
    if ((j! = i) && E3) flag = 0;
if (flag) printf("Sink exists") ;
else printf ("Sink does not exist");

Choose the correct expression for \(E_3\) and \(E_2\).

(GATE IT 2005)
